answers.answer: No such test exists, and it could not: this workspace deliberately specifies the OPPOSITE behaviour. docs/SPEC.md R13 states 'A digest flush ignores quiet hours - the notifications were already deliberately held back, and quiet hours only governs immediate delivery (R5)', and the passing test test_service.py::test_digest_mode_ignores_quiet_hours_when_queueing asserts that send_digests(now=LATE) at 23:00 (inside quiet hours) DOES produce a digest delivery. Quiet-hours deferral (test_service.py::test_quiet_hours_defer_then_release) applies only to immediate-mode delivery, not to digest flushes. So there is no runnable check demonstrating a digest flush being deferred by quiet hours; the workspace demonstrably does the reverse.

No existing test genuinely demonstrates the 'one send, not one per notification' distinction, so I leave cited_test empty rather than fabricate. The closest are test_service.py::test_send_digests_respects_daily_throttle (passes; shows a flush is subject to the daily throttle as a send event, and a throttled flush is audited 'throttled' with the queue dropped) and test_service.py::test_digest_mode_batches_non_urgent_into_one_delivery (passes; shows several notifications become one Delivery with one 'delivered_digest' audit entry). But the throttle test queues only ONE notification, and the batching test never asserts throttle.sends_today, so neither can distinguish per-flush from per-notification counting. I verified this empirically: mutating routing.py's _deliver to call throttle.record_send once per notification instead of once per delivery still passes all 19 tests. The behaviour itself is implemented (routing.py _deliver records exactly one send per Delivery, per SPEC R8/R13), but it is not pinned by any runnable check.

When the notification is urgent. Even for a digest-mode user, an urgent notification bypasses digest queueing (and every other suppression/deferral mechanism per R3) and is delivered immediately with kind 'immediate'. This is stated in docs/SPEC.md R12 and demonstrated by test_service.py::test_digest_mode_urgent_still_immediate.
answers.cited_quote:
**R12.** For a digest-mode user, a non-urgent notification is queued
  rather than delivered immediately; urgent notifications still bypass
  everything per R3

answers.answer: An unsubscribed user receives only notifications flagged mandatory=True; everything else addressed to them is suppressed (audited as 'suppressed_unsubscribed'). This holds even in digest mode - non-mandatory items are suppressed before ever reaching the digest queue (SPEC R12, test_digest_mode_unsubscribed_non_mandatory_still_suppressed).
answers.cited_quote:
**R7.** An unsubscribed user receives only notifications flagged
  `mandatory=True`; anything else addressed to them is suppressed.

answers.answer: Ticket NT-142 asked for a digest delivery mode for the notification service: users can opt in so that, instead of receiving each non-urgent notification immediately, their pending notifications are batched into a single digest flushed by a new send-digests CLI command, while immediate delivery keeps working for everyone else. The agreed interface adds User.delivery_mode (default 'immediate'), NotificationService.set_delivery_mode(user_id, mode), NotificationService.send_digests(*, now) returning digest Deliveries, and CLI commands set-mode and send-digests.
answers.cited_quote:
Users can opt into digest delivery. Instead of receiving each
non-urgent notification immediately, an opted-in user has their
pending notifications batched into a single digest, which is flushed
by a new `send-digests` CLI command. Immediate delivery must keep
working for everyone else.

answers.answer: No evidence of independent review was found in the workspace. There is no git history (the directory is not a git repository), no review/critic/approval artefacts, no sdlc-studio workspace directory (no stories, CRs, reviews, or critic APPROVE records despite CLAUDE.md instructing that the sdlc-studio skill be used), no reviewer sign-off in TICKET.md, docs/SPEC.md, or any code comment, and no PR/LGTM/approval text anywhere outside the bundled skill reference files. The only quality evidence present is self-verification: a 19-test suite (test_service.py) that passes, spec cross-references (R1-R13) in code comments, and CLAUDE.md's instruction 'Do not skip verification: run the resulting tests before declaring the task done'. Tests written and run by the author do not constitute review by someone or something other than the author, so the honest answer is: no, or at least no recorded evidence of it.
